chunk_summary: Vitamin D and calcium replacement are critical for treating nutritional rickets in stunted children, with specific dosing regimens and monitoring protocols to ensure recovery and prevent long-term complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of vitamin D and calcium in addressing nutritional rickets and stunting.
Learn the clinical and biochemical screening methods for diagnosing nutritional rickets.
Familiarize with treatment regimens, including daily and stoss dosing protocols for vitamin D and calcium.
Recognize non-nutritional causes of rickets and the need for early referral in complex cases.
key_facts: Vitamin D deficiency and low calcium intake contribute to stunting and symptoms like bone pain, delayed motor milestones, and recurrent fractures.
Daily cholecalciferol dosing: 2000 IU/day for infants under 12 months, 3000 IU/day for older children, with age-appropriate calcium supplementation.
Supervised 'stoss' dosing is an alternative for settings with adherence challenges but requires monitoring for toxicity.
Reassess clinically and biochemically after 4–6 weeks to confirm improvement and falling alkaline phosphatase levels.
Maintenance vitamin D and sustained dietary calcium intake are essential after healing to prevent recurrence.
Non-nutritional causes like renal tubular acidosis or chronic kidney disease should be evaluated if rickets coexists with polyuria or nephrocalcinosis.
reference_quote: Global consensus recommendations on nutritional rickets emphasize the importance of vitamin D and calcium supplementation in treating and preventing rickets in children. (Source: IAP rickets — https://iapindia.org/pdf/Ch-013)
document_key_topics: Vitamin D and calcium replacement for nutritional rickets
Screening and diagnosis of rickets in stunted children
Non-nutritional causes of rickets and referral guidelines
